summary.ascii_graph:
*---.   Research three unrelated niche topics, extract the requested facts from each Wikipedia page, and then write a synthesis.

1. Cordyceps fungi: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps — find (a) the common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants, (b) one commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps.

2. Admiralty law: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law — find (a) the historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty.

3. Mathematics of paper folding: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding — find (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics, (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding.

Finally, write a 150+ word essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (e.g., hidden complexity, emergent structure, nature inspiring engineering). The essay must reference specific facts from each topic. Cite the Wikipedia URL for every fact.

final_output.final_deliverable:
## 1) Cordyceps fungi (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)

### (a) Common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants
The provided raw page text for **Cordyceps** does **not** contain the requested “common name” for the ant behaviour (e.g., a named behavioural syndrome). The only ant-related phrasing in the retrieved page text is indirect, via a quote about “zombies,” but it does not name a specific behaviour.

Evidence from the visited page shows only this general description:
- From the Cordyceps page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps): Scientific American notes some species “**are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years**”, though “**of ants or tarantulas, not of humans**.”

Because the page text provided here does not include a specific named behaviour (such as a common-name label), that exact fact is **missing from the evidence**.

### (b) One commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps
One documented medicinal use (traditional) is its long-standing role in traditional Chinese medicine.

Evidence:
- From the Cordyceps page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps): “**Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine.**”
- From the “Uses” section (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps): “**Cordyceps sensu lato … has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine in the belief it can be used to treat diseases.**”


## 2) Admiralty law (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)

The required Wikipedia page **was not visited** in the execution trail (it is marked “skipped”), and **no RAW VISIT CONTENT** for it is provided. Therefore, I cannot extract (a) the historical origin/earliest legal code or (b) a modern international maritime convention/treaty with verbatim quotes and citations from the provided evidence.

Missing evidence:
- No page text from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law is included in RAW VISIT CONTENT.


## 3) Mathematics of paper folding (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)

The required Wikipedia page **was not visited** in the execution trail (it is marked “skipped”), and **no RAW VISIT CONTENT** for it is provided. The “Retrieved Memories” show only fragments of outbound links, not the page’s actual statements, so I cannot quote a theorem/axiom or an engineering application from RAW VISIT CONTENT as required.

Missing evidence:
- No page text from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding is included in RAW VISIT CONTENT.


## Synthesis essay (150+ words): A unifying theme—when systems gain power by controlling constraints
Cordyceps, admiralty law, and the mathematics of paper folding can be seen as three domains where the central problem is **controlling constraints**—how a system (biological, legal, or geometric) channels complex outcomes by setting and exploiting rules.

In biology, Cordyceps exemplifies constraint-driven control through parasitism: the Wikipedia page characterizes Cordyceps as “**parasitic**” and notes that “**Most Cordyceps species are endoparasitoids**” that invade hosts (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). Even the cultural description emphasizes a rule-like takeover of behavior—Scientific American is quoted describing species that have been making “**real zombies**” for “**ants**” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). And humans have tried to harness this organismal complexity for their own ends: the page reports “**more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine**” and that it “**has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine**” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps).

Admiralty law and origami mathematics would likely fit the same theme—legal regimes constrain behavior on the seas, and origami axioms constrain allowable folds to produce powerful constructions—but **the necessary Wikipedia evidence for those two topics is missing** here because their pages were not visited and no quotable page text was provided. To complete the synthesis with specific facts (historical legal origins, named conventions/treaties, and origami axioms/theorems plus engineering applications), the RAW VISIT CONTENT from those two Wikipedia URLs is required.
final_output.action_summary: Only the Cordyceps page was actually visited (raw text provided). From it, I extracted a traditional/medicinal use (“more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine” / “used in traditional Chinese medicine”). The specific common-name label for the ant behavior is not present in the provided Cordyceps page text. The Admiralty law and Mathematics of paper folding pages were skipped, so their requested facts and quotes cannot be produced from the given evidence. A 150+ word unifying-theme essay is provided, but it can only cite Cordyceps-specific facts; the other two topics lack visit-based evidence.
